words.shortDefinition: The process of moving or combining a sequence of commits to a new base commit.
words.longDefinition: Rebase rewrites the project history by creating brand new commits for each commit in the original branch. It is often used to keep a feature branch up to date with the main branch, resulting in a cleaner, linear project history.
words.techContext: I performed a rebase onto the main branch to ensure my feature branch included all the latest changes before I submitted my pull request.
words.exampleSentence: Using rebase instead of merge helps us avoid unnecessary 'merge commits' that clutter our history.

codeExample.code:
git checkout feature-branch
git rebase main

words.shortDefinition: Applying the changes introduced by some existing commits to the current branch.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to pick a specific commit from one branch and apply it to another. This is useful for bringing a critical bug fix from a development branch into a stable release branch without merging all the other changes.
words.techContext: We decided to cherry-pick the security patch from the 'v2.0-dev' branch into the 'production' branch.
words.exampleSentence: I had to cherry-pick my colleague's commit because it contained a utility function I needed for my task.
words.etymology: Metaphor for selecting only the best or most desirable items from a group.

words.shortDefinition: Combining multiple commits into a single commit.
words.longDefinition: Often used when merging a feature branch into the main branch. It turns a series of small, incremental commits (like 'fix typo', 'add test') into one clean, meaningful commit, making the project history easier to read.
words.techContext: Our team policy is to squash all commits in a pull request before merging to keep the main branch history concise.
words.exampleSentence: I performed an interactive rebase to squash my last five 'work in progress' commits into one final feature commit.
words.etymology: from Old French esquasser ('to crush, break').

codeExample.code:
git rebase -i HEAD~5
# In the editor, change 'pick' to 'squash' for the relevant commits

words.shortDefinition: Temporarily shelving (or stashing) changes you've made to your working copy.
words.longDefinition: Takes your uncommitted changes (both staged and unstaged), saves them for later use, and then reverts them from your working copy. This allows you to switch branches or perform a pull without losing your current work.
words.techContext: I stashed my half-finished changes so I could quickly switch to the hotfix branch and resolve the production bug.
words.exampleSentence: After finishing the hotfix, I used 'git stash pop' to bring my previous work back into my workspace.
words.etymology: Origin unknown, possibly a blend of 'stow' and 'cache'.

codeExample.code:
git stash
git checkout other-branch
# ... work ...
git checkout original-branch
git stash pop

words.shortDefinition: An event that occurs when Git is unable to automatically resolve differences in code between two commits.
words.longDefinition: Happens when two people change the same line in a file, or one person edits a file while another person deletes it. Git marks the file as conflicted and requires a human to manually choose which changes to keep.
words.techContext: I spent an hour resolving a merge conflict in the configuration file after two feature branches were merged simultaneously.
words.exampleSentence: The merge conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>) showed exactly where my changes overlapped with the main branch.

words.shortDefinition: A snapshot of your changes in the repository's history.
words.longDefinition: When you commit, you are saving the current state of your staged changes to the Git database. Each commit has a unique ID (hash) and a message describing the changes. It is the fundamental unit of version control.
words.techContext: I made a commit with a descriptive message so my teammates would understand why I changed the API endpoint.
words.exampleSentence: You should try to keep your commits small and focused on a single logical change.

codeExample.code:
git add .
git commit -m "Refactor user authentication logic"

words.shortDefinition: A data structure that stores metadata for a set of files or directory structure.
words.longDefinition: Often called a 'repo'. In Git, it contains the entire history of the project, including all commits, branches, and tags. It can be 'local' (on your computer) or 'remote' (on a server like GitHub).
words.techContext: I cloned the repository from GitHub to my local machine so I could start working on the new feature.
words.exampleSentence: Our project repository has grown quite large, so we decided to move some of the older assets to an external storage service.
words.etymology: from Latin repositorium ('a place for storing').

words.shortDefinition: To prepare changes for a commit.
words.longDefinition: Also known as 'the index'. In Git, you don't commit changes directly from your working directory. You first 'stage' the specific files or lines of code you want to include in the next snapshot using the 'git add' command.
words.techContext: I only staged the bug fix, leaving my experimental changes unstaged so they wouldn't be included in the commit.
words.exampleSentence: After staging the files, I ran 'git status' to verify that everything looked correct before committing.
words.etymology: from Latin stagium ('a place for standing').

words.shortDefinition: A command that shows the differences between various states of a repository.
words.longDefinition: It can show the changes between your working directory and the index, between two commits, or between two branches. It provides a line-by-line comparison of added, removed, and modified code.
words.techContext: I used git diff to review my changes one last time before staging them for the commit.
words.exampleSentence: The git diff output showed that I had accidentally deleted a line of CSS that was still needed.

codeExample.code:
# Show changes in working directory
git diff

# Show changes in staged files
git diff --cached

words.shortDefinition: A file that tells Git which files or directories to ignore in a project.
words.longDefinition: Used to prevent temporary files, build artifacts (like 'node_modules'), and sensitive configuration (like '.env') from being tracked by version control.
words.techContext: I added '*.log' to the .gitignore file so my local debug logs wouldn't get pushed to the repository.
words.exampleSentence: We checked our .gitignore to make sure we weren't accidentally committing the large binary data files.

words.shortDefinition: A state in Git where the HEAD pointer refers to a specific commit rather than a branch.
words.longDefinition: Occurs when you checkout a specific commit hash or a tag. In this state, you can look at the code and make experimental commits, but those commits don't belong to any branch and will be lost unless you create a new branch from them.
words.techContext: I ended up in a detached HEAD state because I checked out an old commit to see when a specific bug was introduced.
words.exampleSentence: The warning about a detached HEAD reminded me that I shouldn't try to push any changes until I'm back on a proper branch.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'head' (pointer) that has been separated from its 'body' (branch).

words.shortDefinition: A merge that happens when the current branch has no commits that aren't in the branch being merged.
words.longDefinition: Git simply moves the pointer of the current branch forward to point to the latest commit of the other branch. No new 'merge commit' is created, resulting in a perfectly linear history.
words.techContext: Since no one else had committed to main, my merge was a simple fast-forward that didn't require any conflict resolution.
words.exampleSentence: Fast-forward merges are only possible if the history of the two branches hasn't diverged.

words.shortDefinition: Downloading the latest changes from a remote repository without merging them into your local work.
words.longDefinition: Updates your remote-tracking branches (like origin/main). It's a safe way to see what others have been working on without affecting your own local code. 'git pull' is essentially a 'git fetch' followed by a 'git merge'.
words.techContext: I ran 'git fetch' to see if there were any new commits on the server before I started my own work.
words.exampleSentence: Running fetch allowed me to compare the remote main branch with my local one using git diff.
words.etymology: from Old English feccan ('to bring back').

words.shortDefinition: A method of submitting contributions to an open-source project or a private team repo.
words.longDefinition: Also known as a PR (or Merge Request in GitLab). It tells others about the changes you've pushed to a branch in a repository on GitHub. Once a pull request is opened, you can discuss and review the potential changes with collaborators.
words.techContext: I opened a pull request for the new feature and tagged the senior developer for a code review.
words.exampleSentence: Our pull requests require at least two approvals from other team members before they can be merged into the main branch.

words.shortDefinition: A personal copy of another user's repository that lives on your account.
words.longDefinition: Forks allow you to freely experiment with changes without affecting the original project. They are most commonly used to propose changes to someone else's project or to use someone else's project as a starting point for your own idea.
words.techContext: I forked the open-source library on GitHub so I could fix a bug and then submit a pull request back to the original author.
words.exampleSentence: Forking is the cornerstone of collaborative development in the open-source community.
words.etymology: Metaphor from a 'fork in the road' (splitting into two paths).

words.shortDefinition: A binary search algorithm used to find the commit that introduced a bug.
words.longDefinition: You tell Git a 'bad' commit (where the bug exists) and a 'good' commit (from the past where it didn't). Git then checks out commits in between, asking you if they are good or bad, until it pinpoints the exact commit that broke the code.
words.techContext: We used git bisect to find the exact change that caused the performance regression in our image rendering module.
words.exampleSentence: Git bisect saved us hours of manual searching by narrowing down the problem to a single 10-line commit from two weeks ago.

codeExample.code:
git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD
git bisect good <stable-commit-hash>
# Git checks out a commit, you test it...
git bisect good # or git bisect bad

words.shortDefinition: Creating a new commit that undoes the changes from a previous commit.
words.longDefinition: Unlike 'git reset' (which deletes history), 'git revert' is a safe way to undo changes in a public repository because it adds a new commit to the history rather than removing old ones. It preserves the project's timeline.
words.techContext: I had to run 'git revert' on the broken deployment commit to quickly restore the site to a working state.
words.exampleSentence: Using revert is best for production branches where you want to maintain a clear audit trail of all changes and corrections.

words.shortDefinition: The original repository that you forked or cloned from.
words.longDefinition: In an open-source context, 'upstream' refers to the main project repository. You typically keep your fork in sync with the upstream repository by fetching its latest changes periodically.
words.techContext: I added a remote called 'upstream' that points to the original repository so I can pull the latest security updates.
words.exampleSentence: We need to pull from upstream to make sure our internal changes aren't diverging too much from the main project.
words.etymology: Metaphor from river navigation: moving toward the source.

words.shortDefinition: The default name for the remote repository from which you cloned your local copy.
words.longDefinition: When you clone a repository, Git automatically creates a remote called 'origin' that points back to the server. Most developers push and pull from origin by default.
words.techContext: I pushed my new branch to origin so that my team members could see it on GitHub.
words.exampleSentence: By running 'git remote -v', I confirmed that origin was pointing to the correct URL on our GitLab server.

words.shortDefinition: Scripts that Git executes before or after events such as commit, push, and receive.
words.longDefinition: They allow you to automate tasks and enforce policies. For example, a 'pre-commit' hook can run a linter to ensure that no code with style errors is ever committed to the repo.
words.techContext: We set up a pre-push hook to ensure that the entire test suite passes before code is sent to the server.
words.exampleSentence: The commit failed because our pre-commit hook detected a console.log statement that shouldn't be in the production code.

words.shortDefinition: Switching between different versions of a target entity (usually branches).
words.longDefinition: The command used to switch between branches or restore files. In modern Git (v2.23+), this functionality has been split into 'git switch' (for branches) and 'git restore' (for files).
words.techContext: I checked out the 'bugfix-123' branch to investigate the issue reported by the customer.
words.exampleSentence: Use 'git checkout -b <name>' to create a new branch and switch to it in a single command.
words.etymology: from the concept of checking out a book from a library.

words.shortDefinition: A parallel version of a repository.
words.longDefinition: Branches allow you to develop features, fix bugs, or experiment with new ideas in a contained area of your repository without affecting the main line of development. They are simply pointers to specific commits.
words.techContext: We created a separate branch for the experimental dark mode UI so we could work on it without breaking the main application.
words.exampleSentence: Merging the feature branch back into main was easy because we had been rebasing it regularly.
words.etymology: from Latin branca ('paw, branch of a tree').

words.shortDefinition: A command that displays the history of commits in a repository.
words.longDefinition: Shows the commit hash, author, date, and message for every commit in the current branch's history. It is used to understand the evolution of a project and trace when specific changes were made.
words.techContext: I used git log to see who last modified the authentication service and why.
words.exampleSentence: I customized my git log output to show a simplified, one-line view of the project history with a graph of branches.

words.shortDefinition: Resetting the current HEAD to a specified state.
words.longDefinition: A powerful (and potentially destructive) command that can undo changes by moving the branch pointer back in time. It has three modes: soft (unstages changes), mixed (unstages and removes from index), and hard (completely deletes changes).
words.techContext: I used a 'soft reset' to undo my last three commits while keeping the actual code changes in my workspace so I could squash them.
words.exampleSentence: Be extremely careful with 'git reset --hard', as it will permanently delete any uncommitted work in your directory.

words.shortDefinition: A local record of every time the tip of a branch (or HEAD) was updated.
words.longDefinition: Short for 'Reference Log'. It allows you to recover commits or branches that have been 'lost' due to a hard reset or deletion. It is a safety net for almost any mistake made in a local Git repository.
words.techContext: I thought I had lost my work after a messy rebase, but I found the commit hash in the reflog and was able to restore the branch.
words.exampleSentence: The reflog is unique to your local machine and isn't pushed to the remote server.
